ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Mutton Chukka Lazeez; A0FJP (Mutton, cooked) used as the closest defensible anchor for a pan-fried spiced mutton preparation.

main_ingredients: spices (turmeric, black pepper, cumin, ginger, garlic)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Mutton Chukka Lazeez is approximately 150 g, equivalent to a moderate restaurant portion of dry-cooked spiced mutton pieces served as a side dish or main with flatbread or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Chukka Lazeez — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Chukka Lazeez (cr_id G00010683). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Meat Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on Low-Carbohydrate Spiced Meat Preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Mutton Chukka Lazeez in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate spiced meat dishes. The dish contains only 7.44 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly from onions and tomatoes. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (30 × 7.44 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 3.3; rounded to 2.2 per 100 g equivalent. The low GI is consistent with high protein and fat content slowing gastric emptying and glucose absorption. This is a conservative analogous estimate; direct GI testing has not been performed on this dish.

hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 467 mg/100 g places this dish in the high-concern range for hypertension; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 80–100 g, request reduced salt during preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salted chutneys.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate 7.44 g per 100 g with high protein 14.05 g per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 7.44 g carbohydrate and 4.69 g sugar per 100 g, Mutton Chukka Lazeez has a low glycaemic impact; the high protein content supports satiety and glycaemic stability, making it a suitable protein dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions and not paired with large servings of refined carbohydrates such as white rice or parotta.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 11.58 g per 100 g, cholesterol 85 mg per 100 g, and sodium 467 mg per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of moderate total fat (11.58 g/100 g), cholesterol (85 mg/100 g), and high sodium (467 mg/100 g) warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; portion size should be limited to 100 g, preparation should use minimal added oil, and the dish should be paired with high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the sodium and saturated fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Red meat (arachidonic acid, omega-6) and nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, chilli)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Mutton is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which directly amplifies inflammatory cytokine production relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with RA should avoid or strictly limit this dish and consider substituting with a chicken-based preparation using the same anti-inflammatory spice blend.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Chukka Lazeez is a dry-cooked Indian spiced mutton dish popular in Singapore's Indian restaurant and hawker scene. It is prepared by pan-frying or stir-frying bone-in or boneless mutton pieces with sliced onions, red chillies, tomatoes, and fresh coriander, along with a blend of aromatic spices typically including turmeric, black pepper, cumin, ginger, and garlic. The cooking method is dry (chukka means dry in Tamil culinary terminology), resulting in a dish with a deeply caramelised, spice-coated exterior and tender, flavourful meat. The texture is firm and slightly charred at the edges, with a bold, pungent, and mildly spicy flavour profile. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, 7.44 g carbohydrate, 4.69 g sugar, 0.3 g dietary fibre, and 467 mg sodium. The high protein content makes it a satisfying main protein source. The sodium level is elevated and warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low carbohydrate content makes it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions. The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is typically served as a side dish or main course with parotta, chapati, or rice, and is a staple at Indian Muslim and South Indian restaurants across Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010683) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The record is marked as reviewed and canonical. GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence for GI component only) but all macros and micronutrients available in the HPB record are exact matches.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mutton Chukka Lazeez (G00010683) as of 2026-04-16. No alternative approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN) provides a sufficiently close match for this specific Singapore-style preparation to supplement these values without risk of misattribution. GI value of 30 is an analogous estimate based on low-carbohydrate spiced meat dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database; direct GI testing has not been performed on this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 7.44 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 3.3 per 150 g serving. All core macros (protein_g 14.05, fat_g 11.58, carbohydrate_g 7.44) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
